q3: The model kept defaulting to sequential transitions — 'first, then, finally' framing — even after I pushed back on it multiple times. It understood the critique each time but didn't internalize it structurally, so I was doing the same correction across different paragraphs. It also occasionally slipped into describing what it was doing rather than just doing it, which broke the formal register.
q5: I did look at the rubric, and it captured several things I actually care about — the construct-before-measurement priority and the motivation-before-mechanism structure both align with how I think good methods writing should work. But it missed the thing I care about most at the sentence level: that transitions should express logical dependency, not sequence. 'Causal inter-paragraph dependencies' is close but it's framed at the paragraph level, not the transition sentence level, so the model still produced weak connective tissue even when the paragraph-level logic was sound.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was consistently good at opening paragraphs — the 'why are you reading this' orientation was usually present — but consistently weak at closing them with an argumentative payoff. The rubric names this as 'explicit argumentative payoff' and once I saw it labeled, I noticed the model tended to end paragraphs on procedural detail rather than a claim, which is exactly what the rubric was trying to prevent. So the rubric helped me diagnose a pattern I'd felt but hadn't named.

q3: The model kept treating the payoff sentence as a labeling exercise rather than an illumination exercise — it would correctly identify that a payoff was needed and then write something that described the mechanism instead of conveying why the distinction matters. It took several iterations to get from 'what the system does' to 'why that changes anything.' The motivation-before-mechanism gap also persisted longer than it should have.
q5: I did look at the rubric, and it captured several things I care about reasonably well — the causal dependency criterion and the motivation-before-mechanism criterion both align with how I actually think about paragraph structure. But it didn't fully capture the thing I care most about in the payoff sentence, which is the difference between labeling and illuminating; the rubric says 'conveying the stakes' but the model kept satisfying that criterion on paper while missing it in practice.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model is quite good at satisfying criteria structurally — it would produce a sentence that formally qualifies as a 'payoff' or a 'motivation' without that sentence doing the actual conceptual work. The gap between meeting a criterion and genuinely serving the reader's understanding is something the rubric couldn't fully close, and seeing that pattern play out across iterations was clarifying.

q3: The model kept stalling at the opening sentence — it understood the structural principle (orient before content) but repeatedly asked for clarification rather than committing to a draft, which cost us a full iteration. It also occasionally conflated 'closing the payoff' with 'opening forward pressure' as if these were one move rather than two distinct ones.
q5: I looked at it, yes. The rubric captured several things I care about quite accurately — the motivation-before-mechanism principle and the causal dependency criterion are essentially what I mean by logical flow and orientation. But it doesn't capture the scarcity constraint on plain-language moments, which is actually the harder thing to get right — the rubric says 'restraint is essential' but doesn't enforce it, so the model still risks overusing the technique.
q6: The rubric made me realize the model was consistently stronger on inter-paragraph dependencies than on intra-paragraph structure — it knew how to connect paragraphs causally but sometimes let the internal rhythm of a paragraph flatten into a list. I probably wouldn't have articulated that distinction without seeing the criteria laid out explicitly.

q3: The model struggled with paragraph endings — it kept trying to do two argumentative jobs in one sentence, closing the current point and opening pressure toward the next, which made the transitions feel compressed rather than earned. It also occasionally let the middle paragraphs drift into assertion without showing the mechanism that produced the consequence being claimed.
q5: I did look at it. The rubric captured several things I genuinely care about — the motivation-before-mechanism principle and the causal dependency between units are real priorities for me. But it missed something important: the rhythm I expect in methods sections specifically, that four-beat structure of motivating question, approach overview, formal details, payoff. The rubric gestures at payoff but doesn't capture the internal sequencing I need. So the model got the paragraph-level framing right more often but the section-level architecture still felt slightly off.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was consistently stronger on opening paragraphs than on transitions and closings — it would nail the 'why are you reading this' frame but then lose the thread when it had to both close an argument and create forward pressure. I wouldn't have articulated that asymmetry as clearly without the rubric making the criteria explicit. It also made me notice that the model treated 'strategic plain-language clarity' as a license to insert clarifying sentences rather than understanding that the restraint is the whole point.

q3: The recurring issue was with closing paragraphs — the model kept producing endings that stated what the mechanism does rather than why it matters, and the pruning paragraph in the edge-weighting subsection essentially appeared without causal grounding. It needed explicit feedback to fix the logical dependency between paragraphs rather than getting it right initially.
q5: I did look at the rubric, and it captured several things I care about — particularly the motivation-before-mechanism principle and the causal dependency between units, which map closely to how I actually think about paragraph structure. What it missed, or at least underspecified, is the restraint requirement around plain-language moments — the rubric says 'scarcity' but doesn't convey strongly enough that even one extra instance tips from rigor into casualness. The model still needed my feedback to calibrate that.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was consistently stronger on opening paragraphs than on transitions and closings — it could establish a frame well but then the logical pressure between units would dissipate. I might not have articulated that pattern so cleanly without the rubric making explicit what 'causal inter-unit dependency' was supposed to look like, which let me see more precisely where the model was falling short of it.
